title: Product Quantization (PQ)
concept: Compressing each vector into a compact code by quantizing sub-vector chunks independently, cutting memory drastically.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about the nprobe parameter. What does nprobe control?

options.text: The size of the compressed vector sub-spaces
The number of partitions to search at query time, balancing recall and latency
The total number of clusters created during the indexing phase

exercises.explain: The nprobe parameter controls how many centroid partitions are searched during a query. Higher nprobe increases search accuracy (recall) but also increases search time (latency).

cards.title: The Memory Crunch
cards.body: Vector databases can quickly exhaust RAM when storing millions of large embeddings. Instead of buying expensive hardware, what if we could compress our vectors by 95% or more while still keeping them highly searchable?

cards.title: What is Product Quantization?
cards.body: Product Quantization (PQ) is a compression technique that chops a high-dimensional vector into smaller sub-vectors. Each sub-vector is then matched to the closest center point in a pre-trained dictionary, and replaced by that center's short ID code.

cards.title: The Outfit Wardrobe Analogy
cards.body: Think of Product Quantization like styling an outfit. Instead of describing every thread of your clothes, you pick a shirt color from a small palette, pants from another, and shoes from a third. Together, these three simple category codes define your entire look.

cards.title: Chop, Cluster, Code
cards.body: A 1024-dimensional embedding vector is sliced into 8 chunks of 128 dimensions each. Each chunk is represented by a single byte index (0-255) pointing to a cluster center, shrinking the total storage size from 4,096 bytes down to just 8 bytes!

cards.title: Myth: PQ Quantizes the Whole Vector at Once
cards.body: It actually quantizes independent chunks (sub-spaces) of the vector separately. Quantizing the whole vector at once would require an impossibly large dictionary of options to keep good accuracy.

cards.title: PQ vs Uncompressed
cards.body: Instead of treating a vector as one giant coordinate, PQ slices it and maps each slice to a small codebook.

left.text: Splits vector into 8 segments; stores 8 short index codes (e.g., 8 bytes).

right.text: Stores full floating-point numbers for all dimensions (e.g., 512 bytes).

exercises.prompt: How does Product Quantization (PQ) compress high-dimensional vectors?

options.text: By dropping all negative values in the vector to save space
By splitting vectors into sub-vectors and replacing them with short centroid codes
By converting float values into absolute binary strings of 1s and 0s
By training a neural network to predict the next word in the vector

exercises.explain: PQ splits the vector space into lower-dimensional sub-spaces, trains centroids on each sub-space, and maps each sub-vector to the index of its nearest centroid.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Product Quantization drastically cuts memory usage at the cost of a small loss in vector precision.
exercises.explain: By representing sub-vectors as short codebook indices, memory usage is drastically reduced, enabling massive datasets to fit in RAM.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the description of how Product Quantization breaks down vectors.
exercises.sentence: Product Quantization achieves high compression by slicing vectors into smaller sub-vectors and quantizing them separately.
exercises.explain: Product Quantization works by slicing a long vector into several smaller sub-vectors, and then quantizing each sub-vector independently.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an engineer implementing Product Quantization (PQ)?

options.text: I wrote a Python script to manually truncate all my vector floats to two decimal places to save disk space.
We trained a linear regression model to predict the query latency based on the length of the vector index.
We sliced the 768-dimension embeddings into 12 chunks, mapped each chunk to a centroid index from our codebook, and stored the resulting 12-byte array.

exercises.explain: The third option describes a classic rule-based clustering or vector quantization process with automated ID replacement, while the others describe manual operations or unrelated ML tasks.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you have a 128-dimensional vector using 32-bit floats (512 bytes total). You apply Product Quantization by splitting it into 8 sub-vectors, and run k-means on each sub-space to find 256 centroids (indexable by 1 byte). What is the compressed size of the vector?

options.text: 128 bytes, by reducing the precision of the floats from 32-bit to 8-bit
8 bytes, by using 8 sub-vectors each mapped to a 1-byte centroid index
16 bytes, because the sub-vectors must always use 16-bit float representations
1 byte, because all sub-vectors share a single global centroid index

exercises.explain: PQ divides a 128-dimensional vector into 8 sub-vectors (16 dimensions each). Each sub-vector is replaced by a 1-byte ID pointing to its nearest centroid. 8 sub-vectors times 1 byte each equals 8 bytes total, achieving a massive compression from the original 512 bytes!
